ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic biscuit food product class; no specific Marie biscuit entry exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 category A000E covers biscuits and crackers broadly; no Marie-specific sub-entry exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for Marie biscuit. Omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Marie biscuit (mini) is approximately 20 g, equivalent to about 5 mini biscuits, commonly consumed as a light snack or with tea.

nutrient_source.title: Marie Biscuit (Mini) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Marie biscuit (mini), CR ID F11080036. All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Marie Biscuit — University of Sydney GI Database (analogous: plain sweet biscuit / Marie-type biscuit, GI ~70)
gi_source.source_match_notes: The University of Sydney GI Database lists Marie-type plain sweet biscuits with a GI of approximately 70 (high). The mini format does not alter the GI value as the formulation is identical to standard Marie biscuits. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (70 × 16.13) / 100 ≈ 11.3 for a 20 g serving (16.13 g carbohydrate).

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar (22.58 g/100 g) with zero dietary fibre and elevated sodium (355 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid Marie biscuit (mini) due to its high sugar content (22.58 g/100 g) with no fibre to moderate absorption, which drives insulin-mediated sodium retention, compounded by a sodium level of 355 mg/100 g that contributes meaningfully to daily sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high refined carbohydrate (80.64 g/100 g), high sugar (22.58 g/100 g), zero dietary fibre, and high GI (70)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Marie biscuit (mini) is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes: the combination of 80.64 g/100 g refined carbohydrate, 22.58 g/100 g sugar, zero dietary fibre, and a high GI of 70 produces rapid postprandial glucose spikes, and the mini format significantly increases the risk of unintentional overconsumption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Ultra-processed refined biscuit with high sugar, elevated sodium, and palm-oil-derived saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Regular consumption of Marie biscuit (mini) is inadvisable for individuals with cardiovascular disease, as the product combines high refined sugar (22.58 g/100 g), elevated sodium (355 mg/100 g), and palm-oil-derived saturated fat (~4.2 g/100 g estimated), all of which are independently associated with adverse cardiovascular outcomes.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High refined sugar and starch with zero fibre promote pro-inflammatory mediators
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Marie biscuit (mini) should be avoided by individuals with rheumatoid arthritis, as its high refined sugar (22.58 g/100 g), very high refined carbohydrate load (80.64 g/100 g), and zero fibre promote inflammatory cytokine production, while palm-oil-derived saturated fat may further elevate TNF-alpha activity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Marie Biscuit (Mini) is a small-format version of the classic Marie biscuit — a light, crisp, dry biscuit made primarily from refined wheat flour, sugar, vegetable oil (typically palm oil), and salt, baked to a pale golden colour. The mini format produces bite-sized pieces that are thinner and more delicate than standard Marie biscuits, making them popular as a snack with tea or coffee in Singapore and across Southeast Asia. The texture is characteristically crisp and slightly crumbly, with a mild, subtly sweet flavour and a clean finish. Nutritionally, Marie biscuit (mini) is an ultra-processed refined carbohydrate product: per 100 g it delivers 452 kcal, 80.64 g carbohydrate, 22.58 g sugar, 9.67 g fat, 6.45 g protein, 355 mg sodium, and zero dietary fibre. The absence of fibre combined with a high glycemic index (~70) and very high carbohydrate density makes this product clinically significant for blood glucose management. It is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease, and should be consumed only occasionally and in strictly controlled portions. The mini format increases the risk of overconsumption due to the small individual piece size. It is vegetarian and generally halal-certified in Singapore. Common allergens include wheat and gluten.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Marie biscuit mini?
Is Marie biscuit high in sugar?
Is Marie biscuit suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID F11080036) at confidence 0.95. GI value of 70 is consistent with the University of Sydney GI Database for Marie-type plain sweet biscuits. Saturated fat estimated at ~4.2 g/100 g based on USDA FoodData Central data for comparable plain sweet biscuits (SR Legacy item 18133), documented in notes.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (4.2 g/100 g) is an estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 18133 (Crackers, wheat, plain) as the HPB source does not report saturated fat breakdown for this item; this value should be replaced with a direct HPB or manufacturer figure when available. 2. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific Marie biscuit (mini) product are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of April 2026. 3. Calcium_mg and iron_mg are reported as 0.0 in the HPB source (confirmed zero per HPB Foodo F11080036). 4. Dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 as confirmed by the HPB source; this is a confirmed zero for this highly refined biscuit product. 5. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for Marie-type plain sweet biscuits; the mini format does not alter GI as the formulation is identical. GL per 20 g serving = (70 × 16.13) / 100 ≈ 11.3. 6. This food is not a Singaporean dish and therefore the multilingual naming requirements for Singaporean cuisine (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) do not apply; local_names fields are set to null accordingly.
